sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Rapid growth in the gig economy has been facilitated by the increased use of algorithmic management (AM) in online platforms (OPs) coordinating gig work. There has been a concomitant increase in scholarship related to AM across scientific domains (e.g., computer science, engineering, operations management, management, sociology, and law). However, this literature is fragmented with scholars disagreeing on the conceptualization and measurement of AM, as well as a lack of consensus on the dimensions of AM influencing various gig worker-related outcomes, the mechanisms through which these influences are exerted, and the relevant boundary conditions.

To address these issues, we systematically reviewed the academic literature across scientific disciplines related to the AM of gig workers using natural language processing (NLP)-based topic modeling. Our analysis yielded 12 topics, which we integrate using an input-process-output (IPO) framework to illustrate differing effects of AM on worker-related outcomes. Based on our findings, we provide a comprehensive definition of AM, including its key dimensions, and highlight main mediating pathways through which the individual dimensions of AM impact various gig workerrelated outcomes. Finally, we provide a roadmap for future research on AM in the gig economy (GE) using an organizational behavior lens.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: The past decade has witnessed a rapid growth of the gig economy (GE), which is characterized by flexible, short-term, and on-demand work (Duggan et al., 2020;Meijerink & Keegan, 2019). According to one estimate, around 36% of U.S. workers (approximately 55 million people) are engaged in the GE either to supplement their income or as their main source of sustenance (Gallup, 2018). Central to the growth of the GE are online platforms (OPs), which serve as the intermediaries between gig workers and their customers (Duggan et al., 2023).

These OPs (e.g., Uber, Airbnb, and Fiverr) use algorithmic management (AM), which involves the use of sophisticated artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) algorithms to execute workforce activities (e.g., task allocations, performance evaluations, sanctions) that were previously overseen or coordinated by human managers (Duggan et al., 2023;Kellogg et al., 2020).

Not surprisingly, AM in the GE has attracted a multidisciplinary scholarship (e.g., computer science, engineering, operations management, management, sociology, and law), with research output related to the AM in the GE having increased exponentially over the past decade (see Figure 1 for the trend along with some prominent topics and articles). Furthermore, even traditional organizations are starting to use AM to manage their workforce (e.g., Parent-Rocheleau & Parker, 2022;Sajjadiani et al., 2019). As such, understanding the impact of AM on gig workers may help us to appreciate how employees in traditional organizations (i.e., not OPs) are likely to perceive the replacement of human managers with intelligent, yet impersonal algorithms. However, currently, there is no cross-disciplinary integrated framework detailing the characteristics and effects of AM in the GE (see Table 1 for an overview of existing reviews). Four specific issues arise from this lack of integration. First, despite the many studies related to AM, it is unclear what specific aspects or dimensions comprise this construct as different studies have defined, conceptualized, and measured AM quite distinctly. An inadvertent outcome of these different conceptualizations is that scholars have measured the same underlying construct differently, possibly yielding different results and conclusions when examining the impact of AM on gig worker-related outcomes. Second, there is a gap in our understanding of the mechanisms through which the various dimensions of AM impact gig workers. Although recent reviews, notably Wu and Huang (2024), have proposed mediating mechanisms to explain how engagement in gig work influences important gig worker outcomes, it is unclear how specific dimensions of AM activate these mechanisms, and how these, in turn, affect gig workerrelated outcomes. Borrowing from the strategic human resource management (HRM) literature (e.g., Banks & Kepes, 2015), we refer to these under-examined mediating mechanisms as the "black-box of AM" in the GE. Third, the current literature does not comprehensively address potential boundary conditions for the effects of AM on gig worker attitudes and behaviors (e.g., types of platforms and institutional context). Lastly, despite the relative recency of this research area, a wide array of topics has captured the attention of scholars across scientific disciplines, ranging from various types of ethical and legal issues in platform governance to the impact of AM on gig worker-related outcomes (e.g., well-being, performance, and resistance). However, as noted earlier, the literature appears to lack an interdisciplinary focus, which impedes a comprehensive understanding of important dynamics in the AM-gig worker relation.

Therefore, the purpose of this study is to systematically organize and integrate the fragmented literature across scientific disciplines to understand the effects of AM on gig workers. We do this in the following ways. First, we conduct a comprehensive search across scientific disciplines to ensure that research from all relevant disciplines are included. Next, using natural language processing (NLP)-based structural topic modeling (STM; Roberts et al., 2019), we identify distinct topics within the research domain of AM in the GE. Third, we systematically organize and integrate the identified topics within an F I G U R E 1 Research output related algorithmic management in the gig economy. Note: Documents from 2024 are not included; "in press" documents are included with the year it was first published online representing the publication year. CAGR = Compounded annual growth rate, which represents the annual growth rate (in percent) of the number of articles published over a specific period. CAGR = ((Ending Value/Beginning Value)^(1/n)) À 1, where n represents total time period. The CAGR calculations in our study considers the number of articles published from 2012 until the end of 2023. Thus, CAGR = (((119/1)^(1/11)) À 1)*100 = 54.41%. input-process-output (IPO) framework. Based on this, we provide a comprehensive and holistic definition of AM. Then, building on prior work and using relevant organizational behavior (OB)-related theories, we extend the IPO framework to explore the dynamics within the "black box of AM". Specifically, we examine which specific dimensions of AM trigger which mediating mechanisms and how these affect various gig worker-related outcomes (e.g., well-being, job performance, and resistance). Finally, we propose some additional boundary conditions (i.e., moderators) beyond the ones identified through our literature review that are likely to moderate AM-gig worker relations. We begin by briefly illustrating some of the key aspects of AM in the GE.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Gig work as a concept is not new, and its various forms, often labeled nonstandard work, part-time work, agency work, short-term employment, contingent work, or freelance work, have characterized flexible work systems for the past few decades (Kalleberg, 2000;Tan et al., 2021). However, contemporary gig work mediated by OPs can be distinguished from more traditional forms of nonstandard work on features such as the use of AI-powered algorithms (Duggan et al., 2020), hyper flexibility of work where workers can choose their tasks and work schedule (McDonnell et al., 2021), the use of piecerate type reward systems (Newlands, 2021), and short-duration micro-tasks (Tan et al., 2021). Also, while organizations have traditionally used algorithms to help with the administration of select HRM functions (e.g., Ruël et al., 2004), contemporary applications of algorithms are more "comprehensive, instantaneous, interactive, and opaque than prior workplace technologies" (Kellogg et al., 2020, p. 371). Specifically, algorithms used by OPs rely on comprehensive information from a variety of sources (e.g., gig workers and customers), provide instantaneous feedback (e.g., via calculated ratings), allow for the interactive participation of customers and workers in real-time from different locations, and are opaque in that their inner workings are largely unknown to the workers and customers (Bucher et al., 2021).

Further, OPs use AI-powered ML algorithms that learn from their previous successes and errors with little or no human intervention (Parent-Rocheleau & Parker, 2022). Indeed, there is some evidence that AM has several advantages over human manager-based decision-making, including superior efficiency, clarity, precision, and accuracy (Kellogg et al., 2020;Newlands, 2021;Wiener et al., 2023). Therefore, virtually all OPs in the GE have adopted AM to streamline and automate processes T A B L E 1 Review articles related to the AM in the GE literature.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: • Narrative review of the factors that motivate gig workers to engage in gig work and gig worker individual outcomes.
• Focused on gig work generally; did not elaborate on the different dimensions of AM.
Abbreviations: AI, artificial intelligence; AM, algorithmic management; GE, gig economy; HRM, human resource management; OPs, online platforms.

previously requiring managerial oversight and execution and, thus, considerable labor. However, a thorough reading of the literature indicates that the influence of AM in the GE is complex and goes beyond simple efficiency-based effects. For instance, while gig workers engage with OPs to minimize the effort needed to obtain customers and to experience enriched job-design features such as autonomy and meaningfulness, there is evidence that OP-worker relations are fraught with issues of control, surveillance, and constant pressure to perform (Duggan et al., 2023;Parent-Rocheleau et al., 2024).
An additional challenge is that scholars disagree on what constitutes AM and what its dimensions entail (see

Table 2 for various definitions, conceptualization, and measures of AM). For instance, Möhlmann et al. (2021) viewed AM as comprising algorithmic matching (e.g., matching supply and demand, task allocation, and dynamic pricing) and algorithmic control (e.g., algorithms perform managerial functions, monitoring their activities, and nudging). Similarly, van Zoonen et al. (2023) proposed algorithmic coordination (e.g., task allocation) and algorithmic quantification (e.g., reducing gig workers to numbers) as two dimensions of AM. Cram et al. (2022) also viewed AM as a composed of two dimensions, gatekeeping algorithmic control (e.g., vetting gig workers who want to work for OPs) and guiding algorithmic control (e.g., monitoring and performance evaluation). By contrast, Kellogg et al. (2020) conceptualized algorithmic control as algorithmic direction (e.g., nudging), evaluation (e.g., monitoring and performance rating), and discipline (e.g., gamification of rewards, sanctions, and termination). Zhang et al. (2023), following the work of Pei et al. (2021), conceptualized AM also as a three-dimensional construct, albeit with somewhat different dimensions: specification guidance (e.g., task allocation and performance feedback), tracking evaluation (e.g., monitoring), and behavioral constraints (e.g., rewards and termination). More recently, Parent-Rocheleau et al. (2024) conceptualized AM as a five-dimensional construct (i.e., monitoring, goal setting, scheduling, performance rating, and compensation), noticeably, missing nudging (Möhlmann et al., 2021) and treating dynamic pricing as synonymous with compensation. As these examples illustrate, there is wide disagreement on the scope and dimensionality of the AM construct.

In this paper, we explore the dimensionality of AM and untangle its complex effects by organizing and integrating the existing literature using an IPO model. Originating in the teams literature (Hackman & Morris, 1975;McGrath, 1964), IPO models have been used as organizing frameworks for reviews of literature areas spanning OB, entrepreneurship, and HRM (e.g., Brymer et al., 2014;Hitt et al., 2011;Priesemuth & Schminke, in press;Wright & Snell, 1991). Recently, it was used by Wu and Huang (2024) to describe factors motivating individuals to engage in gig work.

We believe that the IPO framework also lends itself to our domain of inquiry in that it allows us to examine how AM-based inputs, through key mediating processes, affect gig worker-related outcomes. For instance, on the input side, there is evidence that OPs such as ridesharing platforms monitor and control worker behaviors either by directly using algorithms to evaluate and/or direct their behavior in relation to an established standard or through indirect control (e.g., nudging and gamification; Cameron, 2022;Parth & Bathini, 2021). In essence, bureaucratic control, or the "iron cage," is replaced with constant surveillance and evaluation by reputation systems referred to as the "invisible cage" (Rahman, 2021). The mechanisms triggered by AM could include worker-perceived loss of autonomy due to constant surveillance and the pressure to be "on" 24/7, leading to adverse worker outcomes, such as a sense of loss of control and feelings of helplessness, uncertainty, and anxiety (Cheng & Foley, 2019;Parth & Bathini, 2021).

Our model can also incorporate a feedback loop signifying how worker-related outcomes may influence AM-based inputs as the algorithms used by the OPs continuously learn and adapt over time. In our example, gig workers might resist these insidious characteristics of AM by bypassing rules, gaming the system, protesting unfairness, or engaging in collective organizing to force changes in an OP (e.g., changing the reward system and providing more autonomy; Newlands et al., 2018;Tassinari & Maccarrone, 2020). Thus, the use of an IPO framework coupled with the topic modeling approach to organize and integrate the fragmented and siloed literature related to AM in the GE can be viewed as a contribution to this literature area itself. In the section that follows, we describe the details of our literature search and integration process.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: We initially conducted a comprehensive search in the Web of Science (WoS) core collection, one of the most comprehensive sets of databases available (Ampah et al., 2021), to identify relevant documents.

Initially, we searched for GE-related terms (e.g., gig economy, sharing economy, platform economy, platform work*, and gig work*) in the title, abstract, author's keywords, and WoS's keywords. Our search terms were based on previous literature reviews (see Table S1 for the entire list of keywords; Cropanzano et al., 2023;Klarin & Suseno, 2021;Tan et al., 2021). Later, we conducted an additional search using only AM-related keywords (e.g., "algo* management" and "algo* control"). No filters for the year of publication were set.
We restricted our search to articles (including early access), pro-

ceedings, review articles, and book chapters (i.e., we excluded editorial materials, book reviews, and errata), and English language documents. Further, we included both qualitative (e.g., conceptual papers, essays, and ethnographic studies) and quantitative (e.g., studies with data from field surveys) studies pertaining to AM in GE. In addition, we included studies that aligned with the different types of gig work based on Duggan et al.'s (2020) classification as it offers a comprehensive framework to classify gig work based on relevant characteristics. Therefore, we included studies related to app-based gig work (e.g., Uber), crowdwork (e.g., Fiverr), and capital platform work (e.g., Airbnb). Studies focusing on platforms such as YouTube and TikTok were excluded. Aligned with the objective of our review, documents about the GE but not pertaining to AM or aspects of it were excluded. The first two authors developed the inclusion and exclusion criteria and coded the first 50 documents. Interrater agreement was 100%. Given the high degree of agreement, it was decided to continue the coding T A B L E 2 Definitions, conceptualizations, and scales of AM. Study Construct label Definition Dimensionality (number of items) Sample scale items Cram et al. (2022) Algorithmic control An organization's attempt to influence worker behavior by using digital algorithms to enact the control (e.g., to identify a worker behavior that needs to change) and then delivering the control to the worker by a technology interface (e.g., smartphone app) (p. 429). Two-dimensions: • Gatekeeping algorithmic control [GATE] (4) "Screening and monitoring of workers permitted to start or continue working for a company". • Guiding algorithmic control [GUIDE] (9) "Overseeing and guiding dayto-day worker behavior, including performance feedback". • In my opinion, it is hard to get approved to drive for Uber (GATE). • It is difficult for me to comply with all the Uber driver requirements (GATE). • Uber informs me about the activities I am expected to perform (GUIDE). • Uber monitors my driving behavior (GUIDE). • Uber evaluates my driving activities (GUIDE). • Specific performance metrics are established for my job as an Uber driver (GUIDE). • Uber ensures that I, as a driver, am aware of the extent to which I achieve predefined performance goals (GUIDE). Kellogg et al. (2020) Algorithmic control No definition provided. Three-dimensions: • Algorithmic direction (restricting, recommending). • Algorithmic evaluation (recording, rating). • Algorithmic discipline (replacing, rewarding). • Scale items were not developed. Lee et al.
Algorithmic management Software algorithms that assume managerial functions and surrounding institutional devices that support algorithms in practice (p. 1603).
• No specific dimensions.
• Scale items were not developed.

paragraphs: The use of programmed algorithms, often powered by artificial intelligence, by an organization to partially or completely execute workforce management functions and control (p. 27).
Five-dimensions:
• Monitoring (4).
• Goal setting (5).
• Scheduling (4).
• Performance rating (3).
• Compensation (4).

• An automated system tracks me carefully to ensure I am completing my tasks (monitoring). • My daily tasks are assigned by an automated system (goal setting). • An automated system decides when I work and when I do not (scheduling). • The evaluation of my work performance is handled by an electronic system (performance rating).
• A large part of my compensation is determined by an automated system (compensation). van Zoonen et al. (2023)

Based on a reviewer's comment and to ensure comprehensiveness, we extended our search process to the PsycINFO and ProQuest databases. We used the same search terms, supplemented by algorithm-related ones (e.g., algorithmic*), using the AND operator to reduce the large number of documents not relevant to our study.

Finally, we noticed that "in press" articles published on many journal websites have some delay in being indexed by the databases. Therefore, we conducted a manual search on the websites of some prominent OB and management-related outlets (e.g., Journal of Organizational Behavior and Academy of Management Journal) using GE-and AM-related keywords (e.g., "algo* management" OR "algo* control;" see Table S1 for the entire list of journals).
The entire search process was concluded on February 5, 2024.

After removing duplicates, our final sample consisted of 465 documents, 454 from WoS, one from PsycINFO, seven from ProQuest, and three from OB/management-related journals. The entire search and winnowing process is depicted in Figure 2 (for additional information, see Table S1).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: To identify the topics in the AM in the GE literature, we used an NLPbased topic modeling approach. The advantage of such a quantitative approach over traditional narrative reviews is that it is less affected by researcher preferences and subjective choices regarding which topics to include/exclude (Zupic & Čater, 2015). As a result, topic modeling enhances the accuracy of the review process compared to traditional narrative reviews.

In particular, we used the STM algorithm (Roberts et al., 2019) to conduct our analyses. This unsupervised ML technique identifies latent (i.e., hidden) topics within a given corpus (in our case, the corpus is the collection of titles, abstracts, and author-supplied keywords of the 465 documents) without human input. That is, the algorithm identifies and groups words based on their co-occurrence in the given corpus of text to form meaningful topics autonomously.

Like the Linear Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) model (Blei et al., 2003), STM is probabilistic in that the algorithm assumes that each document is a combination of multiple topics, each of which is represented by a group of words that are likely to appear together. We chose STM as our topic modeling approach after comparing its outputs with other similar topic modeling algorithms (e.g., LDA and BERTopic) as the identified topics by STM were more coherent and interpretable. All analyses were conducted in R (version 4.2.2; R Core Team, 2022) with the packages tidytext (version 0.3.4; Silge & Robinson, 2016), quanteda (Benoit et al., 2018), and STM (version 1.3.6;Roberts et al., 2019).

paragraphs: Before conducting topic modeling, it is important to preprocess (i.e., clean) the data to prepare them for the analysis. Our text corpus for analysis was derived by concatenating the title, abstract, and author-supplied keywords. The next steps were to convert our corpus of text to lowercase, identifying the most frequently occurring twoand three-word terms, and merging them to form a single entity (e.g., dynamic pricing was combined to dynamic_pricing), and the elimination of all stop words, such as "a," "an," and "the," which do not have any value when trying to identify topics. Because other words (e.g., "purpose," "methodology," and "results") did not significantly contribute to the identification of topics, we also created a list of custom stop words and conducted an iterative deletion process to determine which words are relevant for the algorithm to identify a particular topic within the AM in the GE literature. Next, we deleted

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Although STM is an unsupervised ML algorithm, it still requires an anticipated number of topics as input information. Research suggests that the topics derived only from statistical optimization techniques tend to have poor interpretability (e.g., Chang et al., 2009;Hagen, 2018). Thus, we followed a mixed approach (Hagen, 2018) where we first derived the optimal number of topics based on the statistical optimization technique (see Figure S1). Subsequently, we manually examined the contents of each topic to ensure its respective interpretability and that the number of topics obtained covered the entire domain of the literature. We believe that our mixed approach enhanced the quality and validity of the identified topics (more information about our methodological approach is in the Supporting Information). The entire list of 465 documents, organized by topic, is available on the Open Science Framework (https://osf.io/26s4v/).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Our STM analysis yielded a final model with 12 topics (see Table 3 and Figure S2). We read the abstracts of all documents associated with each topic and then identified the relevant IPO-related element (i.e., input, process, output, and context) associated with the topic.

We did this by independently reviewing the documents and identifying the main issues and findings in the documents across the 12 topics, discussing them, and then coming to a consensus and, finally, naming each topic. We proceeded only after all authors agreed on the name of each topic and its categorization into the IPO model. Table 3 summarizes the result of this process, including the IPO element, corresponding topic number and name, main keywords (from the 12-topic solution of the STM analysis) and main findings associated with the related topic, and exemplary articles associated with each.

As shown in Table 3, four topics were categorized as inputs, three as processes, and three as outputs. In addition, two topics were classified as context. It should be noted that, given that each document in our text corpus is associated with every topic, several topics also contain words that may be associated with a different part of our IPO model. However, overall, the majority of the words associated with a particular topic can be clearly associated with one part of the IPO model. The resulting model is shown in Figure 3.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: We categorized four topics as inputs in our IPO model of AM in GE. These topics-algorithmic control (Topic 1), pricing strategies (Topic 2), asymmetries (Topic 3), and algorithmic monitoring (Topic 4)-together reflect the influence of AM on workers, constituting the essence of management by OPs. Each of these inputs is described below.

illustrating the search and winnowing process. T A B L E 3 Key topics in research on AM in GE integrated within IPO framework. IPO element Topic number Topic name Main keywords a Main findings Exemplar studies Input 1 Algorithmic control Hrm, human_resource_ management, systems, hrm_activities, autonomy, exploitation, career • Algorithms are performing tasks traditionally handled by HR and line managers. • OPs can exercise direct algorithmic control over gig workers through activities, such as task allocation, performance management, compensation, sanctions, and terminations. • OPs can exercise indirect algorithmic control over gig workers through activities, such as gamification and nudging by targeting workers' unconscious and intuitive mode of thinking. Cameron (2022); Duggan et al. (2020, 2023); Gandini (2019); Norlander et al. (2021); Parth and Bathini (2021) Waldkirch et al. (2021) 2 Pricing strategies Pricing, surge, reputation, demand, dynamic_pricing, autonomy, optimal • Dynamic pricing and surge pricing are used by OPs to address supply-demand gaps and optimize profits. • In the context of Airbnb, pricing strategies have the potential to reduce income disparities among racial minorities. • In the context of ride-hailing platforms (e.g., Uber), opaqueness surrounding AM can be perceived to be unfair to the drivers and their customers. Abrate et al. (2022); Chen et al. (2015); Pignot (2023); Zhang et al. (2021) 3 Asymmetries Power, surveillance, asymmetries, managers, evaluation, reviews, evaluations. • Gig workers lack information regarding algorithms and surveillance activities, resulting in information asymmetry • OPs are able to control the actions of their workers, which creates power asymmetry. • The disparity in the capacity of OPs and gig workers to capitalize on and benefit from the available information results in calculative asymmetry. sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Dynamic or surge pricing constitutes another essential element in the AM of gig workers (Pignot, 2023). It is a strategy used by various ridesharing platforms (e.g., Uber) and other OPs to address supply and demand gaps by temporarily increasing the prices in a particular geographic area, thereby optimizing their profit (Chen et al., 2015;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016). Algorithms are used to determine the exact cost for the customer as well as the profit margins for OPs and the pay for their workers. For instance, information about factors such as the location of the accommodation and seasonal demands can help Airbnb hosts to determine the optimal price for their accommodation (Abrate et al., 2022). Such algorithms may benefit minority hosts who are otherwise at a disadvantage due to factors such as racial, ethnic, or sexual biases and the price sensitivity of their customers (Zhang et al., 2021). In contrast, pricing in ridesharing platforms (e.g., Uber) is entirely determined by algorithms. In this context, the lack of transparency surrounding surge pricing algorithms often leads to their being perceived as unfair to the drivers and their customers as even a slight change in the geolocation can result in quite different surge multipliers, with some customers saving more than half the normal fare while others pay substantially more (Chen et al., 2015).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Asymmetries are inherent part of AM and are deliberately created by OPs to exert direct and indirect algorithmic control over gig workers, as well as to implement pricing strategies (Cutolo & Kenney, 2021;Kellogg et al., 2020;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016;Shapiro, 2020). Studies on this topic have explored three interrelated asymmetries: information (e.g., Cameron & Rahman, 2022;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016), power (e.g., Barbe et al., 2023;Curchod et al., 2020;Cutolo & Kenney, 2021;Heiland, 2022;Le Breton & Galière, 2023;Rahman et al., 2024), and calculative asymmetry (e.g., Shapiro, 2018Shapiro, , 2020)). OPs have access to large amounts of information on the actions of their gig workers, while the latter lack similar information regarding their OP, including their AM (Shapiro, 2018), which creates information asymmetry (Cutolo & Kenney, 2021;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016). Given the unequal distribution of information, OPs can control the actions of their workers, which creates power asymmetry where the OP can unilaterally make important decisions, such as the variance in pay received by workers across different times of the day (i.e., surge pricing, Chen et al., 2015). Calculative asymmetry refers to the disparity in the capacity of OPs and gig workers to capitalize on and benefit from the available information (Shapiro, 2018(Shapiro, , 2020)).

Such asymmetries exist between not only OPs and gig workers, but also customers and gig workers. For example, Rahman and Valentine (2021) found that OPs provide customers (referred to as client manager) with tools to select, evaluate, and reward gig workers, thereby creating power asymmetry between customers and gig workers that has important implications for the task completion. Such asymmetries also exist between gig workers and suppliers (e.g., partner restaurants). As an example, food-delivery drivers' earnings can be impacted by how quickly the restaurants prepare the food.
Additionally, restaurants have an option to rate the food-delivery driver, which creates power asymmetries between suppliers and gig workers (Duggan et al., 2023).

Asymmetries are inherent part of AM and are deliberately created by OPs to exert direct and indirect algorithmic control over gig workers, as well as to implement pricing strategies (Cutolo & Kenney, 2021;Kellogg et al., 2020;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016;Shapiro, 2020). Studies on this topic have explored three interrelated asymmetries: information (e.g., Cameron & Rahman, 2022;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016), power (e.g., Barbe et al., 2023;Curchod et al., 2020;Cutolo & Kenney, 2021;Heiland, 2022;Le Breton & Galière, 2023;Rahman et al., 2024), and calculative asymmetry (e.g., Shapiro, 2018Shapiro, , 2020)). OPs have access to large amounts of information on the actions of their gig workers, while the latter lack similar information regarding their OP, including their AM (Shapiro, 2018), which creates information asymmetry (Cutolo & Kenney, 2021;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016). Given the unequal distribution of information, OPs can control the actions of their workers, which creates power asymmetry where the OP can unilaterally make important decisions, such as the variance in pay received by workers across different times of the day (i.e., surge pricing, Chen et al., 2015). Calculative asymmetry refers to the disparity in the capacity of OPs and gig workers to capitalize on and benefit from the available information (Shapiro, 2018(Shapiro, , 2020)).

Such asymmetries exist between not only OPs and gig workers, but also customers and gig workers. For example, Rahman and Valentine (2021) found that OPs provide customers (referred to as client manager) with tools to select, evaluate, and reward gig workers, thereby creating power asymmetry between customers and gig workers that has important implications for the task completion. Such asymmetries also exist between gig workers and suppliers (e.g., partner restaurants). As an example, food-delivery drivers' earnings can be impacted by how quickly the restaurants prepare the food.

Additionally, restaurants have an option to rate the food-delivery driver, which creates power asymmetries between suppliers and gig workers (Duggan et al., 2023).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: The working conditions experienced by gig workers are characterized by high levels of financial instability, job insecurity, career path uncertainty, work transience, long working hours, limited voice, and physical/relational separation, which can be sources of stress and ill-being (Anwar & Graham, 2021;Caza et al., 2022;Myhill et al., 2021;Sayre, 2023;Wood et al., 2019). They are, at least partly, influenced by AM. As an example, direct algorithmic control (e.g., real-time evaluations and ratings) requires ride-hailing drivers to maintain a minimum performance rating, and those receiving consistently low ratings risk account deactivation. In addition, these drivers are often considered independent contractors (rather than employees), which further exacerbates their sense of job insecurity (Myhill et al., 2021). Similarly, ride-hailing drivers often face uncertainty in their potential earnings due to opaque nature of the task allocation and fare calculation (Rosenblat & Stark, 2016). In addition, studies have highlighted how AM can have a significant impact on workers' voice, thereby influencing their working conditions (Gegenhuber et al., 2021;Kougiannou & Mendonça, 2021). For example, in the context of food delivery drivers, Kougiannou and Mendonça found that AM inhibits their voice because OPs use AM to unilaterally communicate tasks (e.g., following a particular route to deliver an order) with little input from the workers. In response, food-delivery drivers set up alternative voice mechanisms (e.g., social-media platforms) to provide support and advice to fellow drivers.

The described dynamics highlight how AM affects the working conditions of the gig workers. In turn, these working conditions are likely to negatively affect gig worker outcomes, including economic anxiety, distress, and other well-being-related outcomes (Glavin & Schieman, 2022), increase the tendency of workers to take physical risks in order to abide by algorithm-prescribed standards (Bajwa et al., 2018;Christie & Ward, 2019), and ultimately shift the burden of managing safety and health-related burdens from the OPs to the workers themselves (Nielsen et al., 2022).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
sections.header_raw: | Absence of social relations and communities (Topic 6)
sections.header_norm: absence of social relations and communities topic 6
paragraphs: The absence of interpersonal interactions with immediate supervisors and the lack of social interactions with co-workers can negatively impact gig workers' motivation, work and organizational identity, and the development of a sense of meaningfulness in their work (Ai et al., 2023;Schmidt & Van Dellen, 2022). Given the primacy of the customer in this context, workers often go above and beyond job requirements (e.g., doing extra work for free and providing extra amenities or small gifts) to receive higher ratings from customers and/or avoid sanctions by algorithms, which can lead to stress and lowered well-being (Bucher et al., 2020(Bucher et al., , 2021)). However, there is also evidence that virtual shared workspaces provided by online support groups (e.g., Reddit or Facebook groups) can act as a buffer to AM and introduce a sense of community, which is otherwise lacking in the GE (Schmidt & Van Dellen, 2022). Further, workers can develop other types of identification (e.g., hometown and age) to compensate for the lack of an occupational identity (Ai et al., 2023). In addition, gig workers prefer to rely on the experience of their co-workers (and their own judgment) rather than on algorithmic directions (Liu et al., in press), and express algorithmic aversion despite the algorithms' superior predictive capabilities (Dietvorst et al., 2015, p. 114).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Although engagement with OPs is conventionally associated with increased worker flexibility and autonomy (e.g., Milkman et al., 2021;Ruiner & Klumpp, 2023;Sessions et al., 2023;Wiener et al., 2023;Wood et al., 2019), it has also been argued that the use of AM can restrict autonomy (e.g., Duggan et al., 2020Duggan et al., , 2022;;Parent-Rocheleau et al., 2024). For instance, ridesharing drivers are socialized to view themselves as entrepreneurs but often lack any real control over their jobs (Purcell & Brook, 2022); and food-delivery drivers might experience depersonalization due to characteristics of their work (e.g., independent contracting, AM, and no interactions with coworkers; Anicich, 2022). To understand these inherent contradictions surrounding autonomy in gig work (Möhlmann et al., 2021), research has started to explore potential moderators, such as gig workers' perception of OP quality, which is associated with greater autonomy and job satisfaction (Kim et al., 2018), and Theory X versus Theory Y assumptions that shape their entrepreneurial identity and satisfaction with the OP (Ravenelle, 2019). Another moderator might be time itself, as in successive stages of their engagement with the OP, gig workers lose the opportunity to express their voice and influence their OP which is able to continuously experiment with various AM features without informing the workers (Rahman et al., 2023).

paragraphs: Gig workers' interactions with OPs can yield multiple psychological and behavioral outcomes. Our STM analysis identified three key topics that highlight different types of outcomes. Well-being (Topic 8) can be viewed as representing the psychological and physical reactions of workers to their engagement in the GE, while gig worker performance (Topic 9) and resistance (Topic 10) represent different worker behaviors in response to AM.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Well-being-related consequences of gig work have been documented

extensively. For instance, evidence from the ridesharing context indicates that drivers can experience various stressors, including financial uncertainty, damaged reputation from working in low-status jobs, customer incivility, personal boundary violations, and safety concerns (e.g., Beigi et al., 2022). Also, drivers are often objectified as digital symbols on a geographical map with the prime objective of optimizing the time required to complete tasks while ignoring situational factors (e.g., harsh weather conditions and roadblocks) and real-life constraints (e.g., health-related concerns and work-life imbalance issues) (Newlands, 2021).

Not surprisingly, feelings of being monitored constantly have been found to adversely affect gig workers, including their well-being (e.g., emotional exhaustion and sleep deprivation) (e.g., Wood et al., 2019). Other studies have highlighted various concerns related to psychological health (e.g., Anwar & Graham, 2021;Caza et al., 2022;Christie & Ward, 2023;Crain et al., 2020). Similarly, information and power asymmetries can have significant negative outcomes for gig workers, including feelings of powerlessness (Glavin et al., 2021;Shapiro, 2020) and helplessness (Cheng & Foley, 2019), as well as adverse health effects (Gregory, 2021). Finally, pay volatility has been found to create a scarcity mindset among workers and affect their well-being (e.g., headaches and sleep quality and quantity;

Sayre, 2023). However, it has been argued that AM can be designed to improve well-being-related outcomes (e.g., Felix et al., 2023) through nudges or reminders encouraging gig workers to take breaks or by providing stress-management-related tips (Zhang et al., 2022).

Lastly, research has explored various coping strategies to reduce the negative impact of AM on gig workers, including the role of mindfulness (e.g., Sayre, 2023), social support from fellow gig workers (e.g., Nilsen & Kongsvik, 2023), and looking for alternative employment opportunities (e.g., Beigi et al., 2022).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Research is increasingly examining various factors across different levels of analysis (i.e., individual, dyad, and platform) that enhance gig worker performance. At the platform level of analysis, there is evidence linking compensation plans with gig-workers' productivity (Kaynar & Siddiq, 2023) and incentives and penalties or sanctions with the number of hours worked (Xu et al., 2023) and operational performance (Idug et al., 2023). At the dyadic level (i.e., customer-gig worker), there is evidence that perceived interpersonal injustice resulting from lowquality interactions with customers can increase drivers' negative emotions (e.g., anger and unhappiness), which negatively affects their driving and service performance (Lei & Kaplan, 2023). Finally, at the individual level of analysis, transactional (for new drivers) and relational (for long-term drivers) psychological contract-fulfillment positively influences the drivers' task performance both directly and indirectly through organizational identification (Liu et al., 2020). Other studies have explored particular types of performance, such as creative performance (e.g., Bunjak et al., 2021;Schörpf et al., 2017).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: AM in the GE can be viewed as "contested terrain" (Edwards, 1979) where gig workers attempt to gain back some of the power lost to OP through the use of three basic resistance tactics: manipulation or circumvention of algorithms (e.g., faking GPS location to get lucrative rides), subversion (e.g., exploiting loopholes), and disruption (e.g., collective organizing) (Ferrari & Graham, 2021). Manipulation involves breaking OP's rules through varying behaviors, such creating multiple fake accounts to earn more (Grohmann et al., 2022). Subversion comprises the development of "organic algorithms" where workers exploit algorithmic loopholes by, for instance, cooperating with each other (Sun, 2019), screening customers before formally accepting a gig, canceling previously accepted jobs, or filing disputes (Cameron & Rahman, 2022). Finally, disruption involves gig workers leveraging digital media tools (e.g., online forums or instant messaging apps) to organize strikes (e.g., collectively staying offline and stop taking orders for a short period of time; Lei, 2021;Liu & Friedman, 2021). The reasons for collective organizing include low pay and unilateral changes of contract terms by OPs (Lei, 2021) as well as the arbitrariness and lack of transparency of the AM OPs use (e.g., Parth et al., 2023;Soriano & Cabañes, 2020;Tassinari & Maccarrone, 2020).

In addition to these forms of resistance, some studies show that gig workers follow adaptation strategies, such as changing their customer base to prioritize repeat clients (Cameron et al., 2021), attempting to pacify the algorithms by underpricing their work in exchange for favorable reviews, and staying under the radar of the algorithm by avoiding certain topics or keywords during their interactions with the app or customers (e.g., avoiding negative comments about the OP) that might trigger algorithmic scrutiny (Bucher et al., 2021). Furthermore, the use of online forums (e.g., Facebook groups) by gig workers could indirectly help OP maintain existing power dynamics (Le Breton & Galière, 2023). As an example, gig workers' use of online forums facilitates social learning (i.e., gig workers share experiences, integrate others' experiences, and translate these shared experiences into everyday practices). The resulting behaviors align with the expectations of the OP which reinforces the existing power asymmetry rather than resisting it (Le Breton & Galière, 2023).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: There are several social/ethical factors that are of concern to scholars across disciplines. First, issues of digital inequalities persist in the GE where vulnerable populations (e.g., low socioeconomic status and racial/ethnic minorities) have fewer digital skills and, thus, restricted access to digital technologies (Lutz, 2019). Second, personal data collected, stored, and analyzed by OPs are vulnerable to data theft and subsequent identity fraud (Shu et al., 2018). Lastly, there are issues of gender and racial discriminations within customer reviews and ranking systems (e.g., Greenwood et al., 2022;Hannák et al., 2017;Rosenblat et al., 2017;Sühr et al., 2019Sühr et al., , 2021)). For instance, minorities (e.g., Black workers; Hannák et al., 2017) have been found to receive lower ratings on OPs and are ranked lower in search results than similarly qualified White workers; and women ridesharing workers tend to receive stronger penalties than their similarly performing male counterparts (Greenwood et al., 2022).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Scholars are increasingly calling for effective regulatory frameworks (e.g., laws) to address the challenges surrounding OPs' use of AM, which includes measures against surveillance capitalism (i.e., OPs engaging in mass surveillance in their data collection efforts), discrimination, opacity of algorithms, and OPs' unilaterally enforced rules (e.g., de Vaujany et al., 2021;Eyert et al., 2022;Ulbricht & Yeung, 2022). For instance, studies have called for updating consumer protection laws to address issues related to informational opacity and asymmetry that provide OPs with significant advantages over both workers and customers (e.g., Calo & Rosenblat, 2017;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016). Similarly, there is a push for governmental regulation to ensure fairness (e.g., Kullmann, 2018), policies and actions limiting biases (e.g., gender and race) in customer ratings (Rosenblat et al., 2017), and the expansion of employment laws prohibiting discrimination based on demographic attributes to encompass OPs (Leong & Belzer, 2017). Others have suggested that OPs can take active steps in redressing discrimination and establishing equity, such as enforcing fair procedures when customers withhold payments for completed work in labor platforms (e.g., Rani & Furrer, 2021).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: In this study, we reviewed the fast growing, multidisciplinary, and fragmented literature related to AM in the GE. Using NLP techniques, we identified 12 topics within the extant literature and organized, integrated, and discussed these topics using an IPO framework. Our framework highlights the use of AM (i.e., inputs), including algorithmic monitoring, asymmetries (i.e., information, power, and calculative), direct and indirect algorithmic control, and pricing strategies (e.g., surge pricing), to manage gig workers. AM influences gig workers' working conditions, perceived autonomy, and social relations and communities (i.e., processes), thereby affecting various gig worker-related outcomes (e.g., well-being and job performance), including resistance to AM and OPs (i.e., outputs). Based on our findings, we provide several conclusions, which we summarize in an expanded IPO model (see Figure 4). Based on this, we propose directions for future research and discuss implications for practice.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: In our review, we identified four dimensions of AM: Algorithmic monitoring, asymmetries (i.e., information, power, and calculative), algorithmic control (direct and indirect), and pricing strategies (e.g., surge pricing). The current conceptualizations tend not to include all forms of algorithmic control (see Table 2). For example, gamification and nudging are part of indirect algorithmic control used by OPs to subtly influence gig workers' behaviors (Cameron, 2022;Kellogg et al., 2020;Parth & Bathini, 2021), but they are usually omitted from conceptualizations of AM. Similarly, asymmetries, although an essential aspect of AM, are absent from current definitions. Aligned with prior research (e.g., Cutolo & Kenney, 2021;Rahman et al., 2024), we propose that asymmetries are a conscious and deliberate input to and essential feature of AM, rather than an inadvertent outcome. Thus, building on previous work (e.g., Cutolo & Kenney, 2021;Duggan et al., 2020;Kellogg et al., 2020;Parent-Rocheleau et al., 2024;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016;Shapiro, 2020) and the findings of our review, we propose the following definition of AM:

Algorithmic management refers to the use of machine learning algorithms, characterized by algorithmic monitoring activities, the conscious creation of information, power, and calculative asymmetries, algorithmic control involving both direct and indirect control, and dynamically adjusting prices, to manage a workforce.

In this definition, algorithmic monitoring is the first step of AM, facilitating data collection from multiple stakeholders (e.g., customers and gig workers; see Topic 4) to enable the flow of information between other dimensions of AM (e.g., direct, indirect algorithmic control, and pricing strategies). Similarly, pricing strategies (e.g., surge pricing) are a means to addressing demand-supply gaps (Chen et al., 2015;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016), with gamification approaches and nudges facilitating the provision of service in high-demand areas and encouraging task behaviors (Parth & Bathini, 2021). Finally, we view asymmetries as forming a separate dimension that facilitates the implementation of algorithmic control and pricing strategies which, in turn, reinforce pre-existing asymmetries (Ferrari & Graham, 2021;Jarrahi et al., 2021). Based on this comprehensive conceptualization, we recommend that future research develop and validate measures to ensure that the entire domain space of the AM construct is captured.
The input element of Figure 4 reflects the complex interrelations between these different dimensions of AM.

Thus, building on previous work (e.g., Cutolo & Kenney, 2021;Duggan et al., 2020;Kellogg et al., 2020;Parent-Rocheleau et al., 2024;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016;Shapiro, 2020) and the findings of our review, we propose the following definition of AM:

Algorithmic management refers to the use of machine learning algorithms, characterized by algorithmic monitoring activities, the conscious creation of information, power, and calculative asymmetries, algorithmic control involving both direct and indirect control, and dynamically adjusting prices, to manage a workforce.

In this definition, algorithmic monitoring is the first step of AM, facilitating data collection from multiple stakeholders (e.g., customers and gig workers; see Topic 4) to enable the flow of information between other dimensions of AM (e.g., direct, indirect algorithmic control, and pricing strategies). Similarly, pricing strategies (e.g., surge pricing) are a means to addressing demand-supply gaps (Chen et al., 2015;Rosenblat & Stark, 2016), with gamification approaches and nudges facilitating the provision of service in high-demand areas and encouraging task behaviors (Parth & Bathini, 2021). Finally, we view asymmetries as forming a separate dimension that facilitates the implementation of algorithmic control and pricing strategies which, in turn, reinforce pre-existing asymmetries (Ferrari & Graham, 2021;Jarrahi et al., 2021). Based on this comprehensive conceptualization, we recommend that future research develop and validate measures to ensure that the entire domain space of the AM construct is captured.

The input element of Figure 4 reflects the complex interrelations between these different dimensions of AM.
sections.preview_head: In our review, we identified four dimensions of AM: Algorithmic monitoring, asymmetries (i.e., information, power, and calculative), algorithmic control (direct and indirect), and pricing strategies (e.g., surge pricing). The current conceptualizations tend not to include all forms of algorithmic control (see Table 2). For example, gamification and nudging are part of indirect algorithmic control used by OPs to subtly influence gig workers' behaviors (Cameron, 2022;Kellogg et al., 2020;Parth & Bathini, 2021), but they are usually omitted from conceptualizations of AM.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Despite the high volume of research on the topic of AM in the GE, surprisingly little is known about how AM affects gig workers, particularly which dimensions of AM affect which mediating mechanism. This may stem partly from the highly fragmented literature and the dearth of theory-driven research (perhaps with the exception of research on autonomy [Topic 7]), leading to a "black box of AM," which provides ample opportunities for management, particularly OB, scholars to explore the dynamics within this "black box." To provide a starting point for this endeavor and, thus, to identify which dimensions of AM may lead to which particular mediating mechanism, we applied zero-shot topic modeling to our database (see our Supporting

Information for additional details) using general text embedding modeling (Li et al., 2023), implemented as a part of BERTopic (Grootendorst, 2022). This semi-supervised NLP technique is particularly useful for this type of task as it allows us to identify keywords most closely associated with the different dimensions of AM (e.g., monitoring, asymmetries [information, power, and calculative],

direct and indirect algorithmic control [e.g., human resource management, gamification, and nudging], and pricing). As shown in Figure 5, this analysis suggested four categories of mechanisms: affect-, cognition-, job preservation-, and motivation-related mechanisms.

Based on these results, we theoretically linked the different dimensions of AM to gig worker-related outcomes (see Figure 4). In the discussion that follows, we highlight the OB-related constructs and theories that can be used to explain the dynamics within the black box of AM in the GE. We call on future research to build on some of our ideas so that we gain a better understanding of how AM and its dimensions relate to particular outcomes across levels of analysis.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
sections.header_norm: algorithmic monitoring and affect related mechanisms
paragraphs: Algorithmic monitoring of gig workers is likely to trigger affect-related mechanisms (e.g., negative affect, isolation, stress, and anxiety) due to the feeling of being constantly under the gaze of customers and algorithms (Newlands, 2021). This is likely to have adverse effects on the physical and mental well-being of gig workers (e.g., emotional exhaustion, sleep deprivation, and accident risks; see Topic 8). As an example, consistent with job-demand resource theory (Bakker & Demerouti, 2007), the constant monitoring of gig workers is itself a form of high demand. Coupled with the few resources that these workers tend to have to cope with these demands (e.g., the absence of interactions with coworkers), algorithmic monitoring could lead to feelings of anxiety, isolation, and frustration, negatively impacting well-being and other gig worker-related outcomes (e.g., resistance and turnover) (Carlson et al., 2017;Newlands, 2021). Affective events theory (Weiss & Cropanzano, 1996) is another theory that could be used to explain how monitoring causes negative emotions (e.g., stress and frustration), which could then lead to resistance behaviors.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Asymmetries, particularly, information and calculative asymmetries, are associated with (un)fairness, bias, inequality, and discrimination (see Figure 5). Although discrimination and inequality are also an objective reality, subjective assessments of fairness and justice are "cognitively and contextually constructed realities of the user and depend on that person's perceptions" (Shin et al., 2022;p. 2). This indicates a cognitive underpinning of asymmetries associated with gig workers' subjective fairness and justice perceptions. High information (and calculative)
F I G U R E 4 AM in the GE: an expanded integrative input-process-output model.

asymmetries may result in situations where gig workers do not understand algorithmic outputs, which can create cognitive dissonance (Li, in press) as gig workers may hold conflicting cognitions between the algorithmic output and their own experiences. To resolve this conflict and regain cognitive consonance, gig workers may engage in sensemaking processes (Möhlmann et al., 2023;Weick, 1995) by reassessing the algorithmic output and their everyday experiences. If that does not yield the desired consonance, gig workers may display algorithmic aversion by rejecting the algorithmic output (see Topic 6; Liu et al., in press).

Thus, asymmetries are likely to affect gig workers' fairness and justice perceptions (e.g., Colquitt, 2001;Greenberg, 1987), which then influence their attitudes (e.g., job satisfaction), well-being, job performance, and even resistance behaviors. Similarly, a high degree of power asymmetry can negatively affect workers' justice perceptions and perceived lack of control (e.g., voice; Lam & Xu, 2019), likely leading to resistance behaviors.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
sections.header_raw: | Direct algorithmic control and job preservation-related mechanisms
sections.header_norm: direct algorithmic control and job preservation related mechanisms
paragraphs: Direct algorithmic control (e.g., HRM activities) is mainly associated with the job preservation mechanism (see Shoss, 2017), as seen from the keywords "careers" and "employed" (see Figure 5). Gig workers rely mostly on customer ratings and reviews, which are key inputs to HRM activities (e.g., performance appraisals), and may also determine whether gig workers are sanctioned (e.g., due to bad reviews) or even terminated (e.g., if their ratings fall below a certain threshold). The precarious nature of this work (e.g., job insecurity and financial instability; Sayre, 2023), at least partially, arises due to the strong dependence on the combination of customers and algorithms for work and income. As a result, gig workers are likely to engage in emotional labor to display socially desirable behaviors to avoid negative reviews from customers (Bucher et al., 2020(Bucher et al., , 2021)), which can help to protect them from potential future losses. Alternatively, they may engage in adaptation behaviors to pacify the algorithm (see Topic 10)

to avoid negative consequences from the AM. Theories that help to explain the aforementioned dynamics include conservation of resource theory (Hobfoll et al., 2018) and social exchange theory (Blau, 2017).

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
sections.header_raw: | Indirect algorithmic control and motivationrelated mechanisms
sections.header_norm: indirect algorithmic control and motivationrelated mechanisms
paragraphs: As discussed in Topic 1 and based on the keywords from our zeroshot topic modeling analysis (e.g., motivation and engage; see Consistent with goal-setting theory (Locke & Latham, 1990), the indirect control dimension of AM can be in the form of gamification whereby clear and challenging goals are communicated and ongoing feedback is provided to gig workers. In line with self-determination theory (Deci et al., 2017), this could satisfy gig workers' psychological need for autonomy (e.g., voluntary participation in games), competence (reaching a goal), and relatedness (e.g., feelings of belongingness to the OP), thereby increasing their intrinsic motivation (Gera & Hasdell, 2020). Further, the use of nudging as a gentle reminder to reach specific goals can help gig workers mobilize their internal resources to find the most efficient way to achieve their goals, resulting in higher performance and customer satisfaction, and, ultimately, higher earnings (Behl et al., 2021;Cameron, 2022;Gera & Hasdell, 2020).
F I G U R E 5 Zero-shot topic modeling: words associated with each AM dimension. Note: nudging (indirect control) did not form a coherent theme due to its lack of representation in our corpus.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
sections.header_norm: pricing strategies and motivation related mechanisms
paragraphs: As prices for services in the GE directly affects gig worker pay, pricing strategies are likely to influence motivation-related mechanisms in the black box of AM in the GE. For example, consistent with expectancy theory (Vroom, 1964), gig workers expect that their extra efforts (e.g., rides during surge times) will be appropriately rewarded (e.g., higher fare). If these expectations are not met, gig workers are unlikely to be motivated to accept similar jobs in the future and may even discontinue working on the OP. Likewise, according to equity theory (Adams, 1963), gig workers may compare their pay (e.g., during surge pricing) with their past earnings or the earnings of their peers (e.g., during conversations on online social support groups such as Reddit communities). Perceived discrepancies could negatively affect their attitudes (e.g., job satisfaction), leading to resistance behaviors or turnover.

The described theoretical mechanisms identified by our zero-shot topic modeling analysis are largely consistent with recent work on gig workers (e.g., Wu & Huang, 2024). Figure 4 graphically depicts the discussed mechanisms and, therefore, extends the IPO model from our NLP-based STM analysis. However, it is important to note that any one particular AM dimension could activate more than one mediating mechanism. As an example, in addition to cognition-related mechanisms, asymmetries could trigger affect-related mechanisms (e.g., frustration, anxiety) due to the inability of gig workers to understand why the algorithm made a particular decision, which could then affect various gig worker-related outcomes (e.g., well-being and resistance). Although our analysis suggests that some specific mediating mechanisms are more likely than others, the question of which specific aspect of AM affects which mediating mechanism, and how they impact gig worker-related outcomes, are empirical questions that require future research.

As an example, in addition to cognition-related mechanisms, asymmetries could trigger affect-related mechanisms (e.g., frustration, anxiety) due to the inability of gig workers to understand why the algorithm made a particular decision, which could then affect various gig worker-related outcomes (e.g., well-being and resistance). Although our analysis suggests that some specific mediating mechanisms are more likely than others, the question of which specific aspect of AM affects which mediating mechanism, and how they impact gig worker-related outcomes, are empirical questions that require future research.
sections.preview_head: As prices for services in the GE directly affects gig worker pay, pricing strategies are likely to influence motivation-related mechanisms in the black box of AM in the GE. For example, consistent with expectancy theory (Vroom, 1964), gig workers expect that their extra efforts (e.g., rides during surge times) will be appropriately rewarded (e.g., higher fare). If these expectations are not met, gig workers are unlikely to be motivated to accept similar jobs in the future and may even discontinue working on the OP.

paragraphs: The results from our NLP analysis showed that a very limited set of moderators in the AM-gig worker relationship have received attention in literature. For instance, the coping strategies used by gig workers to deal with and manage the negative effects of AM at an intrapersonal level are not well understood. We recommend that future research explore a wider variety of buffering and coping

strategies, including psychological capital (PsyCap; see Luthans & Youssef-Morgan, 2017), job crafting (see Cropanzano et al., 2023), and mindfulness (see Sayre, 2023). As an example, it could be argued that gig workers with high levels of PsyCap are better able to navigate the uncertainties arising from the various asymmetries related to AM. This may help the workers to overcome their experienced isolation stemming from the constant monitoring and very limited social interactions with co-workers and allow them to develop the ability to recover from temporary setbacks. As a result, gig workers with high levels of PsyCap could experience lower levels of stress, anxiety, and burnout. Other potential contextual factors that may moderate the relation between AM and gig workers include the role of national culture (e.g., Wood et al., 2019) and the type of OP (e.g., Pulignano et al., in press), which we also include in our extended IPO model (see Figure 4).

paragraphs: It is important to emphasize that gig workers are not just passive recipients of AM. Instead, as acknowledged in our discussion of Topic 10 (resistance), they actively seek to change the existing dynamics between themselves and their OPs through manipulation, subversion, and disruption. This dynamic interaction creates a feedback loop between the workers and their OPs. A practical application of such a feedback loop can be observed in ridesharing. Initially, information regarding rides and fares were hidden from the drivers working for Uber until they accepted a ride request. The ensuing information asymmetry allowed the OP to exert immense control over its drivers.
However, over time, feedback from Uber drivers prompted the disclosure of some ride and fare information, allowing them to make more informed decisions when accepting or rejecting ride requests.
In many other scenarios, these types of behaviors serve as feedback to OPs, prompting them to update their algorithms to reestablish control over their gig workers (Meijerink & Bondarouk, 2023).
Either way, our understanding of how the resulting changes in AM subsequently impact gig workers' behavior requires empirical research.

paragraphs: Our NLP results showed that resistance is the most frequently studied topic in the literature (i.e., Topic 10; 14% of the topic proportion, see Figure S2), highlighting the need to explore the effect of AM on additional outcomes, including individual (e.g., organizational citizenship behaviors) and organizational outcomes (e.g., reputation). The broadening of the outcome domain may also provide opportunities to examine the effects on customers, such as customer satisfaction or customer incivility, as well as their impact on gig workers' well-being and service performance. Other avenues for future research include examinations of the long-term impact of adaptation behavior (e.g., pacifying the algorithm) on gig workers' counterproductive work behaviors and turnover, the impact of direct and indirect control on creative performance, and spillover effects of gig work experiences on more traditional employee-organization relationships. This latter topic may be of particular importance as more and more employees of traditional organizations are engaging in side hustles in the GE to supplement their income, which can have important spillover effects, both positive and negative (Sessions et al., 2023). More research is needed to understand these effects. For instance, stress and fatigue resulting from constant algorithmic monitoring or hustling for positive reviews or ratings might spill over into workers' experiences and performance in their full-time job (e.g., Karim & Weisz, 2011). At the same time, social and emotional support that employees receive in their regular full-time job could spill over into their side hustles.

sections.header_raw: | Strong negative sentiments around AM in the GE
sections.header_norm: strong negative sentiments around am in the ge
paragraphs: Another conclusion from our review is that the literature on AM in the GE tends to contain strong negative sentiments (an exception is gamification; see Topic 1). To further explore this insight, we conducted a sentiment analysis of all 465 abstracts using the National Research Council (NRC) emotion lexicon (Mohammad & Turney, 2013). The NRC lexicon categorizes words (in our case, the words in the abstracts) into positive and negative sentiments as well as specific emotions (e.g., joy, trust, surprise, anticipation, sadness, anger, fear, and disgust). As shown in Figure S3, the dimensions of AM (e.g., asymmetries) and gig worker-related outcomes (e.g., isolation and discrimination) tend to be associated with negative sentiments and emotions. For example, words such as "discrimination," "asymmetry," "alienation," and "misconduct" are associated with the emotion disgust, a high-valence negative emotion.

Except for a few terms such as "flexibility" and "pay," there are very few words associated with positive sentiments or emotions. Even some of the words categorized under trust, a positive emotion, such as "policy," "legal," "law," and "solidarity," have a strong negative underpinning. As another example, a seemingly positive word such as "solidarity" is often used in the context of resistance, which has clear negative sentiments.

Only relatively recently have theory-driven empirical studies started to emerge. Research in management, particularly OB, has been at the forefront of this very recent development. However, more such research is needed. We hope that our systematic review with the integrated IPO framework and our conclusions based on it help spur more such research in OB and related fields so that we can take an active part in the generation of cumulative knowledge in this vital area of research.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Understanding AM and its impact on gig workers has practical significance for OPs and traditional organizations. Our research provides a unifying framework that identified various topics in the AM in GE literature and discovered specific dimensions of AM that are more likely to produce negative reactions. Our findings can help OPs and traditional organizations to understand AM better and devise strategies to capitalize on some of its positive effects while, at the same time, actively minimize some of the negative ones. As an example, we found that gamification seems to positively influence gig workers by motivating them to work harder to achieve a particular goal. Similarly, nudging, when used properly, can positively influence gig workerrelated outcomes. However, AM dimensions such as the different types of asymmetries seem to have strong negative effects on gig workers. To minimize these, OPs could try to communicate openly and transparently with their workers about the inner workings of their AM (see the idea of "responsible AI"; Barredo-Arrieta et al., 2020). As an example, OPs could provide more explanations (e.g., in the form of notifications) for why gig workers received a particular rating or how their performance evaluations are conducted. Such efforts could go a long way in reducing distrust towards AM and enhance its acceptance. Likewise, OPs could emphasize the use of game-based elements in their AM to enhance gig worker motivation. Relatedly, our review indicates that gig workers who experience problems with their OP often have a need for human interactions to understand and support them. OPs could provide an online forum for gig workers (e.g., online social support)

to discuss some of the issues they face while working for them. In addition, providing gig workers with a means of connecting with a human advisor for grievance redressal could go a long way in enhancing their commitment and satisfaction.

AM is also being used by traditional organizations to increase the efficiency and accuracy of various HRM activities, including job interviews, performance appraisals, and the allocation of financial rewards.

Based on our findings, organizations and managers should be cautious when trying to automate entire HRM activities and HRM-related decision-making processes. Instead, organizations may focus on training their supervisors on AI and ML algorithms as well as on effective ways to communicate and explain to their employees how the algorithms arrived at key decisions. Supervisory relationship-building coupled with the predictive capabilities of ML algorithms is likely to exert a more positive effect on employee outcomes than ML algorithms by themselves. Thus, the combined use of humans and algorithms may provide a "sweet spot" or win-win situation where organizations can take advantage of the inherent advantages of AM while avoiding many of the detrimental effects. Future research should explore what approaches to augmentation may provide this "sweet spot."

As an example, OPs could provide more explanations (e.g., in the form of notifications) for why gig workers received a particular rating or how their performance evaluations are conducted. Such efforts could go a long way in reducing distrust towards AM and enhance its acceptance. Likewise, OPs could emphasize the use of game-based elements in their AM to enhance gig worker motivation. Relatedly, our review indicates that gig workers who experience problems with their OP often have a need for human interactions to understand and support them. OPs could provide an online forum for gig workers (e.g., online social support)

to discuss some of the issues they face while working for them. In addition, providing gig workers with a means of connecting with a human advisor for grievance redressal could go a long way in enhancing their commitment and satisfaction.

AM is also being used by traditional organizations to increase the efficiency and accuracy of various HRM activities, including job interviews, performance appraisals, and the allocation of financial rewards.

Based on our findings, organizations and managers should be cautious when trying to automate entire HRM activities and HRM-related decision-making processes. Instead, organizations may focus on training their supervisors on AI and ML algorithms as well as on effective ways to communicate and explain to their employees how the algorithms arrived at key decisions. Supervisory relationship-building coupled with the predictive capabilities of ML algorithms is likely to exert a more positive effect on employee outcomes than ML algorithms by themselves. Thus, the combined use of humans and algorithms may provide a "sweet spot" or win-win situation where organizations can take advantage of the inherent advantages of AM while avoiding many of the detrimental effects. Future research should explore what approaches to augmentation may provide this "sweet spot."
sections.preview_head: Understanding AM and its impact on gig workers has practical significance for OPs and traditional organizations. Our research provides a unifying framework that identified various topics in the AM in GE literature and discovered specific dimensions of AM that are more likely to produce negative reactions. Our findings can help OPs and traditional organizations to understand AM better and devise strategies to capitalize on some of its positive effects while, at the same time, actively minimize some of the negative ones.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: Our findings should be viewed in the light of an essential limitation.

Although we relied on quantitative techniques to determine the number of topics in the AM in the GE literature, subjectivity was involved in arriving at the 12-topic solution from our STM analysis as well as their categorization into the IPO framework and the selection of the articles to represent each topic. Of these, the placement of each topic in the IPO model is likely the most subjective and there could be arguments to place some topics (e.g., autonomy;

Topic 7) elsewhere (e.g., output). Given that the literature area of AM in the GE is relatively new and highly dispersed and fragmented across several scientific disciplines, this issue was somewhat unavoidable. In our categorization process, we followed conceptual models from the strategic HRM literature where autonomy (e.g., the opportunity to perform) and other individual-level perceptions are part of the black box of strategic HRM (Banks & Kepes, 2015). Still, to address these limitations, we recommend that other quantitative techniques be applied to the AM in the GE literature.

sections.paper_id: Kadolkar et al. - 2025 - Algorithmic management in the gig economy A systematic review and research integration
paragraphs: In this study, we reviewed the literature on AM in the GE and developed a framework to organize and integrate the literature. AM is a new and challenging area of research that has ramifications well beyond the GE; it is actively reshaping how work is and will be performed across virtually all organizational settings. Indeed, AM is starting to get used more and more by traditional organizations.

Hence, it is vital to understand AM's effects on individual employees, groups, and organizations at large. To help in this endeavor, we used NLP-based STM techniques to identify key topics and integrated them into an IPO framework. Our results show a rather complex picture of the use of AM by OPs with a significant impact on gig workers (and, in turn, OPs). We hope that our review spurs much needed theory-driven empirical research to better understand the dynamics in the "black box of AM" and outcomes across levels of analysis.
